1: Theme. Whoever loves discipline loves knowledge, but he who hates reproof is stupid — love of discipline as love of knowledge. Hebrews applies this to divine discipline: the Lord disciplines the one he loves, and chastises every son whom he receives (Hebrews 12:6, citing Proverbs 3:12). The one who loves discipline is the one who has received the son-of-God identity and recognizes the Father's hand in correction. Christ himself learned obedience through what he suffered (Hebrews 5:8) — the fully human son who underwent discipline as the path to completed perfection.
2: Theme. A good man obtains favor from the LORD, but a man of evil devices he condemns — divine favor for the good man. The man of evil devices who is condemned is the figure of the one who plans wickedness — and ultimately of the devil, the ultimate plotter (the evil one throughout John). Christ is the good man (ish tov) who obtained the Father's maximal favor — not merely approval but resurrection, glory, and the name above every name. Those in him are accepted in the Beloved (Ephesians 1:6), participating in his standing before the Father.
3: Theme. No one is established by wickedness, but the root of the righteous will never be moved — the established root. Christ uses the tree-and-root imagery: the tree not planted by the Father will be uprooted (Matthew 15:13); those planted in him have a root that cannot be moved. The metaphor of being established (confirmed, rooted) in Christ appears in Colossians 2:7 (rooted and built up in him) — the immovable foundation Proverbs assigns to the righteous is identified as Christ himself in the NT (1 Corinthians 3:11).
4: Type. An excellent wife is the crown of her husband, but she who brings shame is like rottenness in his bones — the crown-wife as the type of the church. Christ's bride, the church, is the one whom he is sanctifying to present to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing (Ephesians 5:27). The wife who is her husband's crown — his glory, his highest honor in the community — is the type of the church as the glory of Christ (cf. 1 Corinthians 11:7 for this glory-relationship). The rottenness that shames anticipates the unfaithful countertype, elaborated in the Prophets' images of the unfaithful bride (Hos 1-3; Ezek 16).
5: Revelation of God. The thoughts of the righteous are just; the counsels of the wicked are deceitful — just thoughts as the interior of the righteous. Christ's diagnosis goes deeper: the thoughts of the heart are the source of all moral action (Matthew 15:19). His own thoughts were entirely just — in him is no sin (1 John 3:5) at the level of inner deliberation. The sanctification of thoughts and intentions is what Hebrews identifies as the Spirit's work: the word of God... discerning the thoughts and intentions of the heart (Hebrews 4:12).
6: Theme. The words of the wicked lie in wait for blood, but the mouth of the upright delivers them — words that ambush vs. words that deliver. Christ's words deliver those who are being ambushed by false teaching, by guilt, by the accuser. His instruction in the synagogue at Nazareth announces his mission: to proclaim liberty to the captives (Luke 4:18, citing Isaiah 61:1). The mouth of the upright that delivers is the mouth of the one who speaks truth into the situation where ambush has been set.
7: Theme. The wicked are overthrown and are no more, but the house of the righteous will stand — the standing house of the righteous. Matthew 7:24-27 again: the house built on the rock of Christ's words stands when the storm comes; the house built on sand is overthrown. The wicked who are overthrown and are no more is the eschatological pattern — the house of the righteous that stands is the church built on the confession of Christ (Matthew 16:18: on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it).
8: Theme. A man is commended according to his good sense, but one of twisted mind is despised — good sense as the basis of honorable reputation. The sekhel (good sense/wisdom) that produces commendation is the same word used for the wisdom that makes the Servant successful in Isaiah 52:13 (behold, my servant shall act wisely — yaskil). Christ is the Servant whose good sense leads to his exaltation after his humiliation. The commendation according to good sense points to the resurrection-vindication of the one whose wisdom appeared foolish to his contemporaries.
9: Theme. Better to be lowly and have a servant than to play the great man and lack bread — the better-than of honest poverty over pretentious poverty. The incarnation is the maximum expression of this: the Lord of glory became the one who had no place to lay his head (Matthew 8:20), who was served by women from their own means (Luke 8:3), and who washed his disciples' feet (John 13:5) — the one who might have played the great one choosing instead the posture of the servant. The honest acknowledgment of dependence is the posture of those who know they live from grace rather than from performance.
10: Revelation of God. Whoever is righteous has regard for the life of his animal, but the mercy of the wicked is cruel — care for animals as an index of righteousness. God's care extends to animals (Ps 104; Matthew 6:26; Luke 12:6) — the God who notes the sparrow's fall (Matthew 10:29) is the God whose righteous character is reflected in those who care for the creatures under their charge. Christ's shepherd imagery (John 10:11-14) applies the same attentiveness to the sheep that the righteous man applies to his animal — each sheep known by name, each life regarded.
11: Theme. Whoever works his land will have plenty of bread, but he who follows worthless pursuits lacks sense — the work-land-bread connection. The prodigal son squanders everything following worthless pursuits (Luke 15:13 — reckless living) and ends up hungry in a foreign land. His return to the father who has plenty of bread is the parabolic fulfillment of the proverb's reverse: the one who works the father's land has bread; the one who chases emptiness starves. The father's welcome — the fatted calf, rejoicing — is the abundance that waits for the returning worker.
12: Theme. Whoever is wicked covets the spoil of evildoers, but the root of the righteous bears fruit — the rootedness that produces fruit vs. the raiding-for-spoil of the wicked. John 15:5: I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit. The root-bearing-fruit is the vine-branch fruitfulness — the continuous production from rootedness in Christ rather than the episodic raiding that characterizes the wicked's approach to resources.
13: Theme. An evil man is ensnared by the transgression of his lips, but the righteous escapes from trouble — the lip-snare and the righteous escape. Christ's trial demonstrates both: his accusers are ensnared by the false charges they bring — their lips produce the testimony that exposes their own injustice. The righteous one escapes through resurrection — not from suffering (which he enters fully) but from the ultimate death that sin produces. The ensnared accusers receive the judgment they attempted to bring on the innocent.
14: Theme. From the fruit of his mouth a man is satisfied with good, and the work of a man's hand comes back to him — mouth-fruit as self-satisfaction and labor's return. Christ's words produce an overflow that returns to those who receive them: the words that I have spoken to you are spirit and life (John 6:63). The work of his hands on the cross comes back to him in resurrection glory — the fruit of his obedience is the harvest of those given him by the Father (John 17:24). The mouth-fruit-satisfaction principle operates at its maximum in the one whose every word is life.
15: Theme. The way of a fool is right in his own eyes, but a wise man listens to advice — self-assessed rightness vs. counseled wisdom. The disciples' repeated misunderstandings and resistance to Jesus' teaching about his death (Matthew 16:22-23; Mark 9:31-32) — in each case, the way seemed right in their own eyes until Christ reoriented them. Peter's rebuke of the cross-prediction is met with the sharpest correction in the Gospels: get behind me, Satan, for you are not setting your mind on the things of God, but the things of man. The wise man who listens to advice is the one who submits his own assessment to the Christ who sees further.
16: Theme. The vexation of a fool is known at once, but the prudent man ignores an insult — the prudent ignoring of insult. Christ's silence before Herod's questions (Luke 23:9) and his non-defensive responses to the scribes' trapping questions are the model of this prudence. Peter's instruction for those who suffer unjustly: when he was reviled, he did not revile in return; when he suffered, he did not threaten, but continued entrusting himself to him who judges justly (1 Peter 2:23) — the Christ-pattern is precisely the proverb's prudent ignoring of insult, grounded in trust in divine justice.
17: Type / Direct. Whoever speaks the truth gives honest evidence, but a false witness utters deceit — the truthful witness. Christ is the faithful and true witness (ho martys ho pistos kai alethinos, Revelation 3:14; Proverbs 1:5) — the true witness to the Father's character and will. His testimony at his own trial was the truth that condemned him (Mark 14:62) — the one who spoke truth when a convenient lie would have saved him is the maximum truthful witness. Revelation's use of Christ as the faithful witness grounds the call for the martyrs (also martyroi, witnesses) to speak the truth regardless of cost.
18: Theme. There is one whose rash words are like sword thrusts, but the tongue of the wise brings healing — the healing tongue. Christ's words healed: literally (healing the sick, John 9:6-7; Luke 17:14), and spiritually (your sins are forgiven, Luke 5:20). John 6:63: the words I have spoken to you are spirit and life. The tongue of the wise that brings healing finds its ultimate expression in the Logos whose word of command restored what was broken — be clean (Matthew 8:3), arise (Luke 7:14), come forth (John 11:43). Every healing word from Christ is the proverb's healing tongue at its maximum.
19: Revelation of God. Truthful lips endure forever, but a lying tongue is but for a moment — the permanence of truth vs. the momentary duration of the lie. Christ's claim: heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away (Matthew 24:35). The truthful lips that endure forever are fulfilled in the one whose words are eternal — the Logos through whom all things were created and whose speech carries the durability of divine truth. The lying tongue's momentary duration is the wisdom tradition's observation that deception is ultimately unstable and self-dismantling.
20: Theme. Deceit is in the heart of those who devise evil, but those who plan peace have joy — peace-planning as joy-producing. Christ is the Prince of Peace (Isaiah 9:6) and the one who gives peace not as the world gives (John 14:27). The counselors of peace (yoatzei shalom) whose advice leads to joy are the peaceable ones of the Sermon on the Mount: blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God (Matthew 5:9). The joy that belongs to those who plan peace is the beatitude-joy — the kingdom blessing for those whose inner orientation produces shalom.
21: Theme. No ill befalls the righteous, but the wicked are filled with trouble — the righteous's protection from ultimate harm. Christ in John 10:28: I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand. The no ill befalls is not a promise of earthly immunity (the NT explicitly corrects that reading) but of ultimate preservation — nothing can separate the righteous from the love of God in Christ (Romans 8:38-39). The trouble that fills the wicked is the self-generated consequence of the opposition to the God who is their only refuge.
22: Direct / Revelation of God. Lying lips are an abomination to the LORD, but those who act faithfully are his delight — the divine delight in faithful action. Christ is the one who acts faithfully in every respect — the Father's delight in the Son expressed at baptism and transfiguration is the divine delight in the faithfully-acting one. The abomination of lying lips is demonstrated in Christ's contrast with the devil: he was a murderer from the beginning... when he lies he speaks out of his own character, for he is a liar and the father of lies (John 8:44). The delight-abomination distinction is mapped onto the Christ-devil contrast.
23: Theme. A prudent man conceals knowledge, but the heart of fools proclaims folly — prudent concealment of knowledge. Christ instructed his disciples not to reveal his messianic identity prematurely (Matthew 16:20; Mark 8:30) — the knowledge of his identity was concealed until the appointed time of his death and resurrection could make it intelligible. Paul notes that the wisdom of God was hidden for ages until revealed in Christ (1 Corinthians 2:7; Ephesians 3:9-10) — the divine economy of concealment-then-revelation is the maximum expression of the prudent man's practice of withholding knowledge until its proper time.
24: Theme. The hand of the diligent will rule, while the slothful will be put to forced labor — diligent rule. Christ's parable of the talents (Matthew 25:14-30) enacts this principle: the servants who put their talents to work rule over more; the one who buried his talent and was slothful had his resources taken from him and was cast out. The rule that belongs to the diligent is ultimately the co-reign with Christ that faithful servants inherit: you have been faithful over a little; I will set you over much (Matthew 25:21).
25: Revelation of God. Anxiety in a man's heart weighs him down, but a good word makes him glad — the good word as anxiety-cure. Christ's instruction: do not be anxious about your life (Matthew 6:25) — and the gospel is exactly the good word that addresses the root of anxiety. Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. Let not your hearts be troubled, neither let them be afraid (John 14:27). The good word that makes glad is the announcement of the gospel — the word of the cross as good news (euangelion) is the concentrated form of the anxiety-lifting, gladness-producing word Proverbs identifies as the cure for the weighted heart.
26: Theme. One who is righteous is a guide to his neighbor, but the way of the wicked leads them astray — the righteous man as neighbor-guide. Christ is the shepherd who goes before his sheep and they follow him (John 10:4) — the guide whose own path becomes the path of those he leads. The Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep is the maximum expression of the righteous guide: not merely pointing the direction but personally leading through the danger zone. The wicked who lead others astray are the false shepherds who scatter (Ezek 34; John 10:12-13).
27: Theme. Whoever is slothful will not roast his game, but the diligent man will get precious wealth — the follow-through of the diligent. The parable of the ten virgins (Matthew 25:1-13) enacts the follow-through principle: the wise virgins who were prepared with oil had their game roasted; the foolish virgins who failed to carry through with their preparation found the door shut. The precious wealth of the diligent is the inheritance of the kingdom — the eternal life that belongs to those who were found faithful in following through on what they began.
28: Direct / Type. In the path of righteousness is life, and in its pathway there is no death — the path-of-righteousness as deathless life. Christ's declaration: I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live, and everyone who lives and believes in me shall never die (John 11:25-26). The path of righteousness in which there is no death is Christ himself — the Way (John 14:6) in whom those who walk encounter no ultimate death. The proverb is the OT's most direct anticipatory statement of what Christ declares about himself: the righteous path leads to life and the pathway has no death in it.
